title: Login Screen
description: Learn print, input, variables, and if statements by building a working login screen.

steps.title: What You'll Build
steps.content:
Welcome! 👋

By the end of this lesson, you will build a working login screen in Python. It will:

  • Ask the user to type a username
  • Ask the user to type a password
  • Decide if they are allowed in or not
  • Show Access granted or Access denied

Don't worry if you've never coded before. We will go through every part one small step at a time. Each step has a code box you can play with.

Let's start by saying hello. Run the code below by clicking the blue Run button:

✅ If you see Hello! appear, you're ready to begin. Click Next Step at the bottom.

steps.title: print
steps.content:
Showing words on the screen

The word print is how Python shows words on the screen. We use it like this:

print("Hello")

The text we want to show goes inside round brackets and double quotes. Here is what each part does:

print("Hello")
 ⬆    ⬆
this is the word print
             the message in quotes

⚠️ You must use both the brackets ( ) and the quotes " " or it won't work.

Have a go! ✏️

Change the message inside the quotes to your own name, then click Run.
steps.js: PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-print', initialCode: 'print("Hello")', label: 'Have a go!' });

steps.title: Practice: print
steps.content:
Mini Practice 🎯

Use print to show this exact word on the screen:

Welcome

💡 Remember the brackets and the quotes!

steps.title: More than one print
steps.content:
Showing more than one line

You can use print as many times as you like. Each one starts on a new line.

print("Hello!")
print("How are you?")
print("Goodbye!")

This shows:

Hello! How are you? Goodbye!

Have a go! ✏️

Try adding a fourth line that says See you soon!
steps.js: PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-pm', initialCode: 'print("Hello!")\nprint("How are you?")\nprint("Goodbye!")', label: 'Add a fourth line' });

steps.title: Putting things together
steps.content:
Showing more than one thing on a line

If you want to show two things on the same line, put a comma between them:

print("Hello", "there")

This shows:

Hello there

💡 Python automatically adds a space between each thing. You don't need to add it yourself!

This is really useful for joining a label and a value together:

print("Score:", 100)
print("Lives left:", 3)

Have a go! ✏️

Add a third line that says Level: 7
steps.js: PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-pc', initialCode: 'print("Score:", 100)\nprint("Lives left:", 3)', label: 'Try commas' });

steps.title: Practice: Commas
steps.content:
Mini Practice 🎯

Using one print statement with a comma, show this exact line:

Player: Lara

💡 Hint: print("Player:", "Lara")

steps.title: Variables
steps.content:
Storing things to use later

A variable is like a labelled box. You give it a name and put something inside.

name = "Sam"
age = 12

Now Python remembers:

  • The box called name contains "Sam"
  • The box called age contains 12

You can use the box name to show what's inside:

name = "Sam"
age = 12
print("Hello", name)
print("You are", age)
Hello Sam You are 12

⚠️ Notice: no quotes around name in the print. The quotes would make it show the word "name" instead of what's inside the box!

Have a go! ✏️

Change "Sam" to your own name, then click Run.
steps.js:
PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-var', initialCode: 'name = "Sam"
age = 12

print("Hello", name)
print("You are", age)', label: 'Try variables' });

steps.title: input
steps.content:
Asking the user to type something

The word input lets you ask the user a question. Whatever they type, you can save in a variable.

name = input("What is your name? ")
print("Hello", name)

Here is what happens, step by step:

  1. Python shows the question What is your name?
  2. The user types their answer and presses Enter
  3. Python saves what they typed in the box called name
  4. Then it prints Hello followed by their name

💡 When you click Run, the dark output box will show the question. Click on the box and type your name, then press Enter. The output box will get a blue glow when it's waiting for you.

Have a go! ✏️

Click Run, type your name, then press Enter!
steps.js: PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-input', initialCode: 'name = input("What is your name? ")\nprint("Hello", name)', label: 'Try input' });

steps.title: Asking twice
steps.content:
Asking more than one question

You can use input as many times as you need. Each one needs its own variable.

username = input("Username: ")
password = input("Password: ")

print("You typed:", username)
print("And password:", password)

This is exactly what a login screen does! Two questions: a username, then a password.

💡 Click Run, type a username, press Enter, type a password, press Enter again.

Have a go! ✏️

Try anything! For example: username alice, password secret123.
steps.js:
PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-it', initialCode: 'username = input("Username: ")
password = input("Password: ")

print("You typed:", username)
print("And password:", password)', label: 'Two questions' });

steps.title: if
steps.content:
Making decisions

An if statement lets your program make a choice. It only runs some code if something is true.

age = 14

if age >= 13:
    print("You can sign up!")

This says: if age is 13 or more, show "You can sign up!"

⚠️ Two important things:

  • The line ending in : (a colon) is the question
  • The line below must be indented (pushed in with spaces) — this tells Python it belongs inside the if

Here are the symbols you can use to ask questions:

SymbolWhat it asks
==is the same as
!=is different from
>is bigger than
<is smaller than

💡 To check if two things are the same, use two equals signs: ==
One equals sign = means "put this in the box". Two means "is it the same?"

Have a go! ✏️

Change age = 14 to age = 10 and run it. What happens? Then change it back!

steps.title: if and else
steps.content:
What if it's not true?

If you want something to happen when the answer is no, you add an else:

age = 10

if age >= 13:
    print("You can sign up!")
else:
    print("Sorry, you are too young")

Now one of these will always happen:

  • If age is 13 or more → show You can sign up!
  • Otherwise → show Sorry, you are too young

💡 This is exactly what we'll use for the login screen! If the password is correct → let them in. Otherwise → deny them.

Have a go! ✏️

Change age = 10 to a bigger number and watch the message change.

steps.title: Practice: if/else
steps.content:
Mini Practice 🎯

A variable score is already set. Write an if/else that:

  • Shows You win! if score is 50 or more
  • Shows Try again otherwise

💡 Don't change the score line. Your code is tested with both a high score and a low score.

steps.title: The Password Checker
steps.content:
A helper that checks passwords

Checking passwords properly is tricky, so we have written one for you! It is called check_password.

You give it a username and a password. It tells you True (the password is right) or False (the password is wrong).

result = check_password("alice", "Sunshine7")
print(result)
True

🔑 The four valid logins:

  • alice / Sunshine7
  • bob / Football3
  • admin / Adm1n!Pass
  • guest / guest

Have a go! ✏️

Try the code below. Then change the password to something wrong (like "hello") and run it again.

steps.title: Using the checker with if
steps.content:
Putting it together

Now we use check_password with an if/else to show different messages:

result = check_password("alice", "Sunshine7")

if result == True:
    print("Access granted")
else:
    print("Access denied")

Step by step:

  1. Ask the checker if the password is right
  2. It says True or False
  3. If True → show Access granted
  4. If False (else) → show Access denied

Have a go! ✏️

Run it. Then change "Sunshine7" to something wrong and run it again.

steps.title: Challenge: Login Screen
steps.content:
The Big Challenge 🏆

Time to build the full login screen! Your program needs to:

  1. Ask for a username with the question "Username: "
  2. Ask for a password with the question "Password: "
  3. Use check_password to check them
  4. If correct → show Access granted
  5. If wrong → show Access denied

🔑 The four valid logins:

  • alice / Sunshine7
  • bob / Football3
  • admin / Adm1n!Pass
  • guest / guest

💡 The checker function is already at the top of your code. You just need to add the rest below it!

When you click Run, you can type any of the logins above to test it.

steps.title: Endless Mode
steps.content:
Endless Mode ∞

Keep practising with random little challenges! Each one tests something you've learned. Solve one and a new one will appear.

Streak: 0
Best ever: 0
